message.content:
The salt spray had long since bleached Elias’s skin to the color of old parchment, and the rhythm of the waves had become the only clock he trusted. For twenty years, he had tended the Needle, a jagged spire of granite jutting out from the edge of the Blackwood Coast. He was a man of silence, of oil lamps, and of the great Fresnel lens that swept its beam across the churning dark like a blind man’s cane.

It was a Tuesday, or perhaps a Wednesday; the days blurred into a monochrome loop of gray sea and white foam, when the bottle washed up.

It wasn’t like the others. Most bottles were tourist trinkets, glass blown thin and fragile, containing scraps of paper that smelled of cheap perfume or stale beer. This one was heavy, made of thick, green-tinted glass, sealed with wax that had hardened into a dark, amber crust. It had wedged itself deep in the crevice of the rocks below the lighthouse gallery, protected from the worst of the tide.

Elias retrieved it with gloved hands, his heart giving a strange, unfamiliar thump. He carried it up the spiral stairs, the weight of it feeling disproportionate to its size. In the warmth of the lantern room, he cracked the seal. The wax crumbled like dry earth.

Inside was not a note, but a small, folded piece of vellum, and a single, dried sprig of lavender.

Elias unfolded the paper with trembling fingers. The handwriting was elegant, looping, and unmistakably old.

*“To the Keeper of the Light,*

*If you are reading this, the storm has passed, and I have finally found the courage to speak. My name is Elara. I am not a sailor, nor a castaway. I am the daughter of the man who built
